title: The Accusative Case
summary: The accusative case marks the direct object of a verb — the thing or person directly affected by an action. Only masculine singular articles change form; feminine, neuter, and plural forms are identical to the nominative.

sections.heading: Articles in the accusative case
body: In the accusative case, the definite article for masculine nouns changes from der to den, and the indefinite article changes from ein to einen. All other forms — die (feminine), das (neuter), and the plural die — remain the same as in the nominative. The same pattern applies to the negative kein: masculine kein → keinen.

To identify the direct object, ask 'Wen?' (whom?) for people or 'Was?' (what?) for things. For example: 'Ich kaufe den Apfel.' → 'Was kaufe ich? Den Apfel.' — den signals that Apfel (masculine) is the direct object.

examples.en: She has no brother. (masc. indef. neg.)

sections.heading: Accusative prepositions
body: Certain prepositions always require the accusative case. The most important ones are: durch (through), für (for), gegen (against / around a time), ohne (without), um (around / at a time). A helpful mnemonic is 'durch für gegen ohne um'.

After any of these prepositions, masculine articles must change to the accusative form (den / einen). For example: 'für den Mann', 'ohne einen Stift', 'durch den Park'. Feminine and neuter forms look the same as the nominative, but they are still grammatically accusative.

examples.de: Das Geschenk ist für den Vater.
examples.en: The present is for the father.
We are walking through the park.

examples.de: Das Auto fährt um die Ecke.
examples.en: The car turns around the corner.

sections.heading: Personal pronouns in the accusative
body: Personal pronouns also change in the accusative case. The key changes are: ich → mich, du → dich, er → ihn, sie (she) → sie, es → es, wir → uns, ihr → euch, sie (they) / Sie (formal) → sie / Sie.
Note that er changes to ihn (parallel to der → den), which is the most important pronoun change to remember.

exercises.explanation: Schlüssel is masculine. As the direct object it takes the accusative form: der → den.

exercises.question: Which form is correct? "Das Geschenk ist ___ meine Mutter."
exercises.explanation: für (for) is the correct accusative preposition here. Die Mutter is feminine so the article stays die: für meine Mutter.

exercises.prompt: Er kauft ___ neuen Mantel. (ein Mantel – masculine)
exercises.explanation: Mantel is masculine. The indefinite article in the accusative is einen.

exercises.question: "Ich sehe ___ (er) morgen." Which pronoun is correct?
exercises.explanation: The pronoun er changes to ihn in the accusative case (direct object).
durch takes the accusative: den Wald (masculine). Word order: subject (Wir) – verb (laufen) – prepositional phrase (durch den Wald).
